title: Designing Controls Well
subtitle: Design vs operating effectiveness

hook.metaphor: A smoke alarm screwed to the ceiling but with no battery is *well-designed and totally useless*. A great control has to be two things at once: designed right AND actually working. Auditors have separate words for these — and the gap between them is where most control failures hide.
hook.scenario: A bank's policy says all wire transfers over ₹10 lakh need dual approval. The design is textbook. But in practice, the second approver is on leave half the year and payments just… go through anyway. Is this control effective?

learn: Two distinct questions: **design effectiveness** — *if this control operated as intended, would it actually address the risk?* — and **operating effectiveness** — *is it actually operating that way, consistently, in real life?* A control must pass both. Our wire-transfer control is well-designed but operating-ineffective (the battery's out).

This is the **documented-vs-implemented** gap again (it haunts every GRC theme): a beautiful policy that nobody follows fails on operating effectiveness, even with perfect design. Both halves get tested, and both can fail independently.

A practical tool for getting design right is the **risk-control matrix (RCM)**: list each significant risk, then the control(s) that address it, the owner, and how it's evidenced. It exposes two dangers — **gaps** (a risk with no control) and **over-control** (five overlapping controls on a trivial risk while a big risk sits naked).

And every control needs a named **control owner** — the person accountable for it actually running. 'Everyone's responsible' means no one is. The owner makes sure the smoke alarm has a battery, that the second approver has a backup, and that the control keeps working when people go on leave or move on.
bridge: So at work, you can interrogate any control with two separate questions, not one: *Is it designed to address the risk?* and *Is it actually operating that way every day?* And you can spot trouble fast by asking who the **owner** is — a control without a clearly accountable owner is a battery waiting to die.

interactions.title: Designed vs working
interactions.prompt: Dual approval is required for big wires, but the second approver is often away and payments clear regardless. Verdict?

options.label: Effective — the policy clearly requires dual approval.
options.feedback: That's design effectiveness only. In practice the control doesn't operate — payments clear without the second approval. It fails operating effectiveness; the design on paper is cold comfort.

options.label: Design is fine, but it's operating-ineffective — fix the real-world execution.
options.feedback: Exactly. The control is well-designed but not actually working. The fix is operational: a backup approver, a hard system block, an accountable owner — so it runs even when someone's on leave.

options.label: Just delete the policy since it's not followed.
options.feedback: The control addresses a real risk (large unauthorised wires) — you don't remove it, you make it actually operate. Deleting it leaves the risk fully exposed.

interactions.title: Which kind of failure?
interactions.prompt: Sort each problem by whether it's a design flaw or an operating failure.

items.label: The control wouldn't catch the risk even if performed perfectly
A well-designed check is skipped when staff are busy
No control exists for a significant risk (a gap)
The approver signs without actually reviewing
The control only covers half the transactions it should

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “A good control must be both designed right and actually working — a smoke alarm with no battery passes design but fails in practice — so you test both, map risks to controls, and give every control an owner.”

checkpoint.q: The difference between design and operating effectiveness is…
options: Design = would it work if performed; operating = is it actually performed that way
Design is for big firms, operating for small
checkpoint.explain: A control must pass both: sound on paper AND consistently performed in reality.

options: Gaps (risks with no control) and over-control (too many on trivial risks)
checkpoint.explain: Mapping risks to controls, owners, and evidence exposes both uncovered risks and wasteful duplication.

checkpoint.q: Why does every control need a named owner?
options: Because 'everyone's responsible' means no one ensures it keeps working
checkpoint.explain: An accountable owner keeps the control operating through leave, turnover, and change — the battery stays in.
